subtopics.title: Measuring National Income
common_misconceptions: GDP and GNP are the same (GNP = GDP + net property income from abroad)
Higher GDP always means better standard of living
National income can only be measured one way
subtopics.teaching_note: GDP: total value of goods/services produced within a country in a year. GNP: GDP + net income from abroad. NNP: GNP - depreciation. Three approaches: Income (sum of all incomes), Expenditure (C+I+G+(X-M)), Output (sum of value added). Real GDP = (Nominal GDP/Price Index) × 100. Per capita income = National Income/Population. Nigerian context: GDP ~$390 billion (2024), largest in Africa. Dependent on oil (80%+ of export earnings). GDP rebasing in 2014 showed services (Nollywood, telecoms) larger than oil. Challenges: accurate measurement due to informal sector (estimated 60%+ of economy).

subtopics.title: Inflation - Causes and Control
common_misconceptions: All inflation is bad (mild inflation ~2% can encourage spending/investment)
Inflation only affects the poor (erodes savings for all)
Printing more money has no effect on inflation
subtopics.teaching_note: Inflation: sustained increase in general price level. Measured by CPI (Consumer Price Index) or GDP deflator. Demand-pull: too much money chasing too few goods. Cost-push: rising production costs (wages, raw materials, oil prices). Effects: reduced purchasing power, menu costs, shoe-leather costs, redistribution (hurts fixed income earners, benefits debtors). Control: monetary policy (CBN raises interest rates, reduces money supply), fiscal policy (higher taxes, reduced spending), supply-side policies, price controls (subsidies). Nigerian context: Inflation ~25-30% (2024). Causes: food price increases, naira devaluation, fuel subsidy removal, insecurity affecting food production. CBN's monetary policy rate (MPR) adjustments.

subtopics.title: Theories and Terms of Trade
common_misconceptions: Absolute advantage means producing everything better (comparative advantage matters for trade)
Trade surplus is always good (can indicate under-consumption)
subtopics.teaching_note: Comparative advantage: countries should specialize in goods with lowest opportunity cost, then trade. Terms of trade: (Index of export prices/Index of import prices) × 100. TOT > 100: favorable. Balance of payments: current account + capital account + financial account. Exchange rate systems: fixed, floating, managed float. Nigerian context: Naira floats (managed). Persistent trade deficit in non-oil sector. Trade restrictions: tariffs (import duties), quotas (import bans on rice, poultry at times), import licensing. ECOWAS Trade Liberalization Scheme (ETLS). African Continental Free Trade Area (AfCFTA).
